---
title: "AI Crawler Observatory · Italy"
format:
  html:
    theme: [cosmo, theme.scss]
    page-layout: article
    toc: false
    anchor-sections: false
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

```{python}
import json
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import HTML
import charts

meta = json.load(open("data/meta.json", encoding="utf-8"))
snapshot = meta["snapshots"][-1]
n_snapshots = len(meta["snapshots"])

# The parquet tables accumulate every snapshot; these sections read the latest.
df = pd.read_parquet("data/policy_facts.parquet").query("snapshot_date == @snapshot")
sig = pd.read_parquet("data/sector_signals.parquet").query("snapshot_date == @snapshot")
res = pd.read_parquet("data/reserved_domains.parquet").query("snapshot_date == @snapshot")


def as_table(t, filename):
    """Table view of a chart's data — same numbers, no hover needed, and
    downloadable as CSV."""
    return HTML(charts.table_html(t, filename))

n_domains = int(sig["n_domains"].sum())
n_sectors = len(meta["sectors"])
n_crawlers = len(meta["crawlers"])
snapshot_label = (f"snapshot {snapshot}" if n_snapshots == 1
                  else f"snapshot {n_snapshots} · {snapshot}")
```

::: {.kicker}
AI Crawler Observatory · Italy
:::

::: {.proposition}
A recurring census of what Italian websites tell AI crawlers.
:::

::: {.lead}
In Italy, resistance to AI crawlers is concentrated in one sector: the press.
:::

News blocks OpenAI's GPTBot at 31% and media at 21%; no other sector exceeds 10%.

```{python}
HTML(charts.signal_cards(df, sig, res))
```

::: {.dateline}
`{python} n_domains` domains · `{python} n_sectors` sectors · `{python} n_crawlers` crawlers · `{python} snapshot_label` · [Method →](#method)
:::

## Block {#block}

Sites keep AI crawlers out through **robots.txt** — a plain-text file that can
name a crawler and disallow paths. Where that happens is uneven: below, the
share of each sector's sites that block GPTBot *by name*.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Chart

```{python}
charts.block_by_sector(df)
```

### Table

```{python}
as_table(charts.block_table(df), "block-by-sector.csv")
```

:::

Crawler by crawler, the picture is the same one seen from the other side. Each
bar is one crawler and splits into how the 543 sites treat it: **blocked** at
the root, **partial** (some paths off limits), or **allowed**. Every bar is a
whole, so the figures inside it are shares of sites, not counts — the counts are
in the tooltip and the table. Use the menus to slice by sector, or by whether a
rule *names the crawler* or comes from a catch-all `*`.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Chart

```{python}
charts.verdict_by_crawler(df, default_source="specific")
```

### Table

```{python}
as_table(charts.verdict_table(df), "verdicts-by-crawler.csv")
```

Rules that name the crawler — the chart's opening view; use the chart's menus
for the other slices.

:::

## Offer {#offer}

The opposite move. **llms.txt** (a 2024 convention) is a Markdown file that
*offers* a curated view of a site to language models — "here is what to read
about us." Almost nobody uses it: **46 of 543 sites, 8.5%**. And the lead is
an unexpected sector — **banking**, where more than 1 in 4 sites publish one,
and every one is hand-written, not shipped by a plugin.

The chart below counts only those 46 publishers — not the whole sample — and
splits each sector's bar by where the file came from: ink for a hand-written
file, gray for a plugin default. That split is the question this section asks:
how much of the adoption was a decision rather than a side effect. Sector
sample sizes are in the table.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Chart

```{python}
charts.offer_by_sector(sig)
```

### Table

```{python}
as_table(charts.offer_table(sig), "llmstxt-by-sector.csv")
```

:::

## Reserve {#reserve}

The legal one. Under EU copyright law (Directive 2019/790, Article 4), AI
companies may text-and-data-mine public content for training **unless the owner
reserves the right in a machine-readable form**. **TDMRep** is that reservation —
the only one of the three signals with legal weight in the EU. Almost nobody
uses it, and those who do live on copyright.

```{python}
HTML(charts.reserve_stat(sig, res))
```

Every site that reserves, and the channel it signals through:

```{python}
as_table(charts.reserve_table(res), "tdmrep-sites.csv")
```

## Method {#method}

**The sample.** `{python} n_domains` Italian websites, stratified across
`{python} n_sectors` sectors, drawn from hand-curated sector lists cross-checked
against the Tranco top-1M list filtered to `.it`. Latest snapshot:
`{python} snapshot`. The observatory is built to re-run: each crawl is one
snapshot, so the same measures can be tracked over time and, later, across
countries.

**The three signals.** For every site we fetch and *validate* three files:

- **robots.txt**, parsed under RFC 9309 (longest-match, `Allow` wins ties). A
  crawler's verdict is **blocked** when the root path is denied, **partial**
  when only some paths are, **allowed** otherwise. A rule is *specific* when the
  site names the crawler, *wildcard* when it comes from a catch-all
  `User-agent: *`.
- **llms.txt**, a Markdown file offering content to models. *Hand-written* vs
  *plugin default* is inferred from generator signatures and near-duplicate
  detection across sites.
- **tdmrep.json / TDMRep**, the EU legal reservation, read through all three
  spec channels (a well-known file, HTTP headers, and HTML meta tags).

**Presence is validated, never assumed.** A `200 OK` proves nothing — many
servers answer 200 with an HTML page on any path. A file counts only if it is
structurally valid; soft-404s are excluded.

**Limits.** robots.txt measures *declared intent*, not crawler behaviour. The
CMS/plugin fingerprint is heuristic. "Not mentioned" is a default, not a
decision, and is kept separate from explicit permission throughout.

**Data & code.** `{python} n_crawlers` AI crawlers are tracked. The full pipeline
and the aggregated snapshot data are open:
[github.com/mxdangelo/aicrawl-census](https://github.com/mxdangelo/aicrawl-census).

```{=html}
<script>
(function () {
  // Sticky dateline: a sentinel just above it tells us when it is pinned,
  // so the hairline/background only appear once it actually docks.
  var dl = document.querySelector('.dateline');
  if (!dl || !('IntersectionObserver' in window)) return;
  var sentinel = document.createElement('div');
  dl.parentNode.insertBefore(sentinel, dl);
  new IntersectionObserver(function (entries) {
    dl.classList.toggle('is-stuck', !entries[0].isIntersecting);
  }).observe(sentinel);
})();
(function () {
  var targets = document.querySelectorAll('main section.level2');
  var reduce = window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').matches;
  if (!('IntersectionObserver' in window) || reduce) return;   // graceful: stay visible
  targets.forEach(function (el) { el.classList.add('reveal'); });
  var io = new IntersectionObserver(function (entries) {
    entries.forEach(function (e) {
      if (e.isIntersecting) { e.target.classList.add('is-visible'); io.unobserve(e.target); }
    });
  }, { rootMargin: '0px 0px -8% 0px', threshold: 0.06 });
  targets.forEach(function (el) { io.observe(el); });
})();
</script>
```
